planting.note: Plant root divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; peppermint is a sterile hybrid that does not breed true from seed — propagate by vegetative division or cuttings. Contain in pots or with barriers as it spreads by rhizomes.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; peppermint is a natural hybrid between water mint (M. aquatica) and spearmint (M. spicata), producing the highest menthol content of any common mint and the distinctive, strong, cooling flavor used in candies, chewing gum, toothpaste, teas, and culinary applications worldwide. The plant spreads vigorously by underground stolons — grow in sunken containers or surrounded by physical barriers to prevent it from taking over the garden. It prefers partial shade and consistent moisture; in hot, dry, full-sun conditions it becomes stressed, loses potency, and is more susceptible to spider mites. Harvest before flowering for peak flavor and essential oil content. In zone 9, peppermint grows best in the cooler months of fall through spring, becoming less vigorous in the intense heat of summer.

common_name: Camphor Basil (African Basil, East African Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; camphor basil is a fast-growing, shrubby tropical basil that becomes woody at the base in warm climates. In zone 9–10 it may perennialize, but most gardeners treat it as an annual.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; camphor basil is a species from East Africa (particularly the slopes of Kilimanjaro, as the name suggests) with a strong, sharp, camphor-eucalyptus fragrance unlike any other basil. The essential oil is composed primarily of camphor and 1,8-cineole rather than the linalool or estragole that characterize most familiar culinary basils. In East Africa, the plant is used medicinally for respiratory conditions (inhaling the vapors), as an insect repellent, and as a flavoring ingredient in certain traditional foods. It has been used as a parent in breeding disease-resistant basil hybrids, contributing resistance to Fusarium wilt. A larger, woodier, more shrubby plant than culinary sweet basil, it makes an impressive ornamental with a strong fragrance when brushed. Bees and butterflies are attracted to the white flower spikes.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination or sow indoors in late winter; white deadnettle grows in moist to moderately dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun, and self-sows to naturalize in appropriate conditions. Plant in shade where it provides spring ground cover.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; white deadnettle is a European mint-family herb with nettlecreeper-shaped leaves (which it resembles but it does not sting — hence 'deadnettle') and conspicuous white hooded flowers throughout spring and early summer. The white flowers, with their distinctive hood and nectar guides, are exclusively pollinated by long-tongued bumblebees that can reach the nectar at the base of the long corolla tube — shorter-tongued bees cannot access it. The plant has a long history of use in European herbal medicine, with the flowers and leaves used as an anti-inflammatory herb, wound herb, and menstrual regulator. Young leaves and flowers are edible in salads. Spreads moderately by rhizomes and self-sows to form a semi-wild presence in the shade garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or very early spring in any well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; red deadnettle is a winter annual that germinates in fall, overwinters as a rosette, and flowers in early spring before dying back. Self-sows prolifically once established.

directions: Sow in any garden soil in fall or early spring in partial shade to full sun; red deadnettle is a common European winter annual now naturalized worldwide, growing as a charming winter and early spring wildflower in lawns, gardens, and disturbed areas. The small, purple-hooded flowers, which appear from late winter through spring, are one of the earliest nectar sources for queen bumblebees emerging from winter dormancy — a service of significant ecological value. The purplish-tinged young leaves are edible raw in salads or cooked as a pot herb, high in vitamins and minerals. The plant germinates in fall and overwinters as a low rosette, making it valuable as a living mulch and green cover during winter months. It does not compete with summer crops and dies naturally as temperatures rise. Embrace this plant as a beneficial winter ground cover rather than treating it as a weed.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; sunset hyssop is a species from the desert Southwest and Mexico, adapted to dry, lean, alkaline soils in full sun. In zone 9 it may be short-lived but self-sows in appropriate conditions. Does not tolerate wet winter soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sunset hyssop is a southwestern Agastache with unusually bright orange to flame-red flowers — the most vivid color in the genus, quite different from the lavender and purple typical of most anise hyssops. The nectar-rich, tubular flowers are magnetically attractive to hummingbirds and the flowers and leaves have a fruity, licorice-mint fragrance used in herbal teas. Several popular garden cultivars including 'Apricot Sunrise', 'Peachie Keen', and 'Sunset Hyssop' derive from this species. Drought tolerant once established; prefers lean soil over rich, organic beds where it tends to be short-lived. In zone 9, the combination of heat tolerance, hummingbird attraction, and fragrant foliage makes it an excellent late-summer nectar plant.
